subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will explore the world of three-dimensional shapes – the solid shapes we call 3D shapes for short. Look around you right now. The box that holds Amina's dates, the ball Yusuf kicks in the football match, the tin of tomatoes on the shelf at the Hargeisa market, the cone of an ice-cream at Eid – every one of them is a 3D shape. Unlike flat shapes drawn on paper, 3D shapes take up real space in the world. You can pick them up, turn them around, and hold them in your hands.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, hold a real object in your hand while you read, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to describe a shape in a new way, give you extra practice, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Understanding 3D shapes helps you make sense of the world around you. Builders must know shapes to raise a strong house. Traders stack boxes that are cuboids so they fit neatly on the shelf. A potter shaping camel-milk jars is working with cylinders and spheres. When you can name a shape and describe its parts, you are learning the language that engineers, architects and designers use every day – and you are building the foundation for measuring volume and surface area in later years.

As you work, keep some real objects beside you: a tin, a box, a ball, a party hat, a dice. Mathematics with shapes is best learned by touching and turning. Run your finger along an edge. Press your fingertip on a corner. Feel the flat and curved surfaces. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here makes you a sharper, more confident mathematician.
Identify and name common 3D shapes: cube, cuboid, sphere, cylinder, cone, prism and pyramid.
Describe a 3D shape by its faces, edges and vertices.
Explain the difference between a prism and a pyramid.
Recognise 3D shapes in everyday objects around you.
Sort and classify 3D shapes by their properties.
Understand and use nets – the flat patterns that fold into solid shapes.
Sketch simple 3D shapes and label their features.

concepts.title: What Makes a Shape 3D?
concepts.explanation: A flat shape, like a square or a circle drawn on paper, has only two measurements: how long it is and how wide it is. We call these 2D shapes, which is short for two-dimensional. You can draw them but you cannot pick them up off the page. A 3D shape is different. It has three measurements: length, width and height. Because it has height as well, it stands up off the page and takes up space in the world. The word for the space a solid shape takes up is volume. A drawing of a box on paper is 2D, but the real box…
concepts.example: Every solid 3D shape can be described using three special features. Learn these three words well, because you will use them in every single lesson of this unit:

concepts.title: The Common 3D Shapes
concepts.explanation: Now let us meet the family of common 3D shapes one by one. As you read about each, try to picture a real object from your home or the market that has that shape. Holding the real thing while you read makes the shape stick in your memory. A cube has 6 faces, and every single face is a square of exactly the same size. It has 12 edges, and all twelve are the same length. It has 8 vertices. A dice is a perfect cube, and so is a sugar cube or a child's building block. Because all its faces are equal squares, a cube…
concepts.example: A cuboid also has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices, just like a cube. But on a cuboid the faces are rectangles, and they are not all the same size. A cereal box, a book, a brick, and the box that Hassan's new shoes came…

concepts.title: Counting Faces, Edges and Vertices Carefully
concepts.explanation: Counting the faces, edges and vertices of a shape sounds easy, but it is very simple to lose your place and count the same edge twice, or miss a hidden one at the back. Good mathematicians count in an organised way so nothing is missed and nothing is counted twice. Let us learn a careful method using a cube. To count the faces of a cube, imagine the cube as a box. Count the top and the bottom – that is 2. Count the front and the back – that is 2 more, making 4. Count the left and the right – that is the last 2,…
concepts.example: To count the edges, count the 4 edges around the top square, then the 4 edges around the bottom square – that is 8 so far. Then count the 4 upright edges that join the top to the bottom. That gives 8 + 4 = 12 edges. To…

concepts.title: Understanding Prisms
concepts.explanation: A prism is a very special kind of 3D shape. A prism has: Two identical ends that are exactly the same shape and the same size. These ends are called the bases.
concepts.example: Flat faces all around – a prism has no curved surfaces.

concepts.title: Understanding Pyramids
concepts.explanation: A pyramid is another special 3D shape, but it is built in a very different way from a prism. A pyramid has: One base, which can be any flat polygon – a triangle, a square, a pentagon and so on.
concepts.example: Triangular faces that rise up from the edges of the base and all meet at a single point.

concepts.title: Sorting and Classifying 3D Shapes
concepts.explanation: Because there are so many 3D shapes, mathematicians like to sort them into groups. Sorting means putting shapes together that share a property. There is no single right way to sort – it depends on which property you choose. Here are some useful ways to sort the shapes you have met: Shapes with only flat faces (cube, cuboid, prisms, pyramids) versus shapes with at least one curved surface (sphere, cylinder, cone).
concepts.example: Shapes with at least one triangular face (triangular prism, pyramids) versus shapes with no triangular faces (cube, cuboid, cylinder).

explorations.title: What Makes a Shape 3D?

explorations.prompt: Name each shape: a) has 6 square faces b) is perfectly round with no edges c) has a circular base and one point at the top.
explorations.answer: 1) a) cube, b) sphere, c) cone.
explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.
explorations.explanation: 1) a) cube, b) sphere, c) cone.

explorations.answer: 1) a) cube: 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 vertices; b) cuboid: 6, 12, 8; c) cylinder: 3, 2, 0; d) cone: 2, 1, 1.

methods.title: Look at the faces: are they flat, curved, or both?
methods.example: A shape has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices, and all its faces are squares. What is it?
steps: Count the faces in an organised order (top, bottom, sides).
Count the edges where faces meet.
Count the vertices (corners).
Decide: is it a prism, a pyramid, or neither? Then name it.

methods.title: How to tell a prism from a pyramid
methods.example: How many faces, edges and vertices does a triangular prism have?
steps: Look for two identical ends. If they are there, it is a prism.
Look for a single point at the top (an apex). If it is there, it is a pyramid.
Name the shape after its base: triangular, square, hexagonal, and so on.

methods.title: How to use a net
methods.example: How many faces, edges and vertices does a triangular prism have?
steps: Imagine unfolding the shape until it lies flat.
Count the flat faces you can see in the net.
Fold it back in your mind to check it makes the solid shape.

methods.title: Identify from a real object
methods.example: What 3D shape is a tin of tomatoes at the market?
steps: Solution: A tin has a flat circle at the top, a flat circle at the bottom, and a curved surface around the side.
A shape with two circular faces and one curved surface is a cylinder.
Check the identify from a real object result carefully.

methods.title: Use the base to name a prism
methods.example: A prism has two identical hexagonal (six-sided) ends. What is its name?
steps: Solution: Prisms are named after the shape of their base.
The base is a hexagon, so the shape is a hexagonal prism.
Worked Example 6: Curved or flat?
Sort these into curved-surface shapes and flat-face-only shapes: cube, sphere, cone, cuboid, cylinder.

methods.title: Curved or flat?
methods.example: A prism has two identical hexagonal (six-sided) ends. What is its name?
steps: Solution: Prisms are named after the shape of their base.
The base is a hexagon, so the shape is a hexagonal prism.
Worked Example 6: Curved or flat?
Sort these into curved-surface shapes and flat-face-only shapes: cube, sphere, cone, cuboid, cylinder.

workedExamples.title: Name the shape from its features
workedExamples.prompt: A shape has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices, and all its faces are squares. What is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Six faces, twelve edges and eight vertices could be a cube or a cuboid – those numbers are the same for both. The extra clue is that all the faces are squares. Only a cube has all-square faces, so the shape is a cube.

workedExamples.title: Count the parts of a triangular prism
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces, edges and vertices does a triangular prism have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count in an organised way. Faces: 2 triangular ends + 3 rectangles joining them = 5 faces. Vertices: 3 corners on the front triangle + 3 corners on the back triangle = 6 vertices. Edges: 3 around the front triangle + 3 around the back triangle + 3 joining them = 9 edges. Worked Example 3: Prism or pyramid? Hassan has a shape with a square base and four triangular faces meeting at a point. Is it a prism or a pyramid, and what is its name? Solution: It has one base and comes to a single point (apex), so it…

workedExamples.title: Prism or pyramid?
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces, edges and vertices does a triangular prism have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count in an organised way. Faces: 2 triangular ends + 3 rectangles joining them = 5 faces. Vertices: 3 corners on the front triangle + 3 corners on the back triangle = 6 vertices. Edges: 3 around the front triangle + 3 around the back triangle + 3 joining them = 9 edges. Worked Example 3: Prism or pyramid? Hassan has a shape with a square base and four triangular faces meeting at a point. Is it a prism or a pyramid, and what is its name? Solution: It has one base and comes to a single point (apex), so it…

workedExamples.title: Identify from a real object
workedExamples.prompt: What 3D shape is a tin of tomatoes at the market?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A tin has a flat circle at the top, a flat circle at the bottom, and a curved surface around the side. A shape with two circular faces and one curved surface is a cylinder.

workedExamples.title: Use the base to name a prism
workedExamples.prompt: A prism has two identical hexagonal (six-sided) ends. What is its name?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Prisms are named after the shape of their base. The base is a hexagon, so the shape is a hexagonal prism. Worked Example 6: Curved or flat? Sort these into curved-surface shapes and flat-face-only shapes: cube, sphere, cone, cuboid, cylinder. Solution: A cube and a cuboid have only flat faces. A sphere, cone and cylinder each have at least one curved surface. So: flat only = cube, cuboid; curved = sphere, cone, cylinder.

workedExamples.title: Curved or flat?
workedExamples.prompt: A prism has two identical hexagonal (six-sided) ends. What is its name?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Prisms are named after the shape of their base. The base is a hexagon, so the shape is a hexagonal prism. Worked Example 6: Curved or flat? Sort these into curved-surface shapes and flat-face-only shapes: cube, sphere, cone, cuboid, cylinder. Solution: A cube and a cuboid have only flat faces. A sphere, cone and cylinder each have at least one curved surface. So: flat only = cube, cuboid; curved = sphere, cone, cylinder.

workedExamples.title: Count the net
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf unfolds a shape and sees 1 square and 4 triangles lying flat. Which shape did it come from?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: One square base and four triangular faces fold up to a single point. That is a square-based pyramid.

workedExamples.title: Compare two shapes
workedExamples.prompt: Write one thing that is the same and one thing that is different about a cube and a square-based pyramid.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Same: both have flat faces and both have a square somewhere (the cube has square faces; the pyramid has a square base). Different: a cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices, while a square-based pyramid has only 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices – and the pyramid rises to a point while the cube does not.

workedExamples.title: True or False
workedExamples.prompt: True or False: a) a sphere has one curved surface b) a cone has two vertices c) a cube is a special cuboid.
workedExamples.solution: a) True, b) False (0 vertices), c) True.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A prism has two identical ends shaped like pentagons. What is its name and how many rectangular side faces does it have?
workedExamples.solution: A pentagonal prism; it has 5 rectangular side faces.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Which shapes have at least one triangular face: cube, triangular prism, square-based pyramid, cylinder?
workedExamples.solution: Triangular prism and square-based pyramid have triangular faces.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A shape has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. One face is a square and the rest are triangles. Name it and describe it.
workedExamples.solution: 1) A square-based pyramid: it has 1 square base and 4 triangular faces meeting at an apex, with 8 edges and 5 vertices.

practice.prompt: A prism has two identical ends shaped like pentagons. What is its name and how many rectangular side faces does it have?
practice.answer: A pentagonal prism; it has 5 rectangular side faces.

practice.prompt: Which shapes have at least one triangular face: cube, triangular prism, square-based pyramid, cylinder?
practice.answer: Triangular prism and square-based pyramid have triangular faces.

practice.prompt: A shape has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. One face is a square and the rest are triangles. Name it and describe it.
practice.answer: 1) A square-based pyramid: it has 1 square base and 4 triangular faces meeting at an apex, with 8 edges and 5 vertices.
practice.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

activities.title: Shape Detectives
steps: Walk around your home and the courtyard looking for 3D shapes.
For each shape you find, write down the name of the shape, the object it is part of, and how many faces, edges and vertices it has.
Try to find at least ten different 3D shapes.
Challenge: Sort your shapes into groups. How many different ways can you group them – by flat or curved, by can roll or cannot roll, by prism or pyramid?
This activity trains your eyes to see the mathematics hidden in everyday objects.

activities.title: Build-a-Shape with Straws and Clay
activities.materials: straws or thin sticks for the edges, and small balls of clay, dough or dates for the vertices.
steps: Use straws as edges and clay balls as vertices.
Build a cube, then a triangular prism, then a square-based pyramid.
For each shape, count how many straws (edges) and how many clay balls (vertices) you used, and write them down.
Challenge: Can you build a shape with exactly 9 edges? What about exactly 6 vertices? (Hint: think about the triangular prism.)

activities.title: The Shape Feely Bag
activities.materials: a cloth bag or pillowcase, and several small 3D objects (a dice, a tin, a ball, a small box, a party hat).
steps: Put the objects into the bag without letting your partner see.
Your partner reaches in, feels one object, and describes its faces, edges and vertices out loud – without looking.
From the description, they must name the shape before pulling it out to check.
Swap over and take turns. Who can name the most shapes just by touch?
Feeling shapes with your hands helps you truly understand faces, edges and vertices.

activities.title: Make a Net (Investigation)
activities.materials: an empty cardboard box (a small cereal or tea box), scissors, and a pencil.
steps: Carefully open the box along its glued edges until it lies completely flat. Ask an adult to help with the cutting.
Look at the flat shape – this is the net of the box. Count how many rectangles you can see.
Draw around your flattened box on paper to record its net, then fold it back up into the cuboid.
Challenge: Can you draw a net for a cube using 6 squares? Cut it out and fold it to test whether it works.

activities.title: Prism to Pyramid (Family Task)
activities.materials: straws and clay, and a family member to work with.
steps: Together, build a triangular prism. Count the straws and clay balls you used (you should need 9 straws and 6 clay balls).
Now change it into a triangular pyramid by removing straws and clay. Count what you use now (6 straws and 4 clay balls).
Talk about how the two shapes are the same and how they are different.
Challenge: Try the same with a square base. Build a shape with a square base as a prism, then rebuild it as a square-based pyramid, and compare.

activities.title: 3D Shape Property Game
activities.materials: a dice, some paper shape cards you draw yourself, and counters (stones or bottle caps).
steps: Write a property on each face of a paper key: all faces flat, has a curved surface, has a triangular face, has a square face, has no vertices, has an apex.
Roll the dice and read the matching property from your key.
Place a counter on one shape card that has that property. Each card can hold only one counter.
Keep playing until all the cards are covered. The winner is the player with the most counters.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand faces, edges and vertices best? What was the trickiest shape to build? Which shape can you now name and describe most confidently? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Prisms are named after the shape of their bases. This is a very useful rule, because once you can name the base you can name the whole prism. For example, a triangular prism has triangular bases; a rectangular prism (a cuboid) has rectangular bases; a hexagonal prism has six-sided bases. Even a cube is a prism – its bases are squares, so it is really a special square prism. When Ibrahim looks at a new prism, the first thing he does is look at the two matching ends and ask, What shape are they? That answer names the shape.

terms: A surface of a solid; flat or curved
A line where two faces meet
One base, faces meet at an apex
The point at the top of a pyramid or cone
A flat pattern that folds into a 3D shape
commonMistakes: Face = surface, edge = line, vertex = corner
Count in order; include what you cannot see
Curved surface is not a face
Faces depend on the base shape

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo market, Sahra stacks tins of tomatoes and boxes of dates. Explain which shape stacks most easily and why, using the words faces and edges.
realProblems.answer: 1) The boxes (cuboids) stack most easily because they have flat faces that sit flat on each other and straight edges that line up; the round tins can roll.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) The boxes (cuboids) stack most easily because they have flat faces that sit flat on each other and straight edges that line up; the round tins can roll.

realProblems.prompt: During Ramadan, Abdi arranges dates in a box shaped like a cuboid and camel milk in a tin shaped like a cylinder. For each, write the number of faces, edges and vertices.
realProblems.answer: Cuboid box: 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 vertices. Cylinder tin: 3 faces, 2 edges, 0 vertices.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Cuboid box: 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 vertices. Cylinder tin: 3 faces, 2 edges, 0 vertices.

realProblems.prompt: Liban builds the great pyramid model for a school project. It has a square base and four triangular faces. How many faces, edges and vertices does his model have?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Noor finds three objects: a globe, a Toblerone box, and a sugar cube. Name the 3D shape of each and say which one has no vertices at all.
realProblems.answer: Globe = sphere (no vertices), Toblerone box = triangular prism, sugar cube = cube. The sphere has no vertices.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Globe = sphere (no vertices), Toblerone box = triangular prism, sugar cube = cube. The sphere has no vertices.

realProblems.prompt: Maryam is describing a shape to Hani without naming it. She says: It has two circular faces and one curved surface, and it can roll on its side but stands on its ends. What shape is she describing?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A shape has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. One face is a square and the rest are triangles. Name it and describe it.
realProblems.answer: 1) A square-based pyramid: it has 1 square base and 4 triangular faces meeting at an apex, with 8 edges and 5 vertices.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) A square-based pyramid: it has 1 square base and 4 triangular faces meeting at an apex, with 8 edges and 5 vertices.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A shape has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. One face is a square and the rest are triangles. Name it and describe it.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) A square-based pyramid: it has 1 square base and 4 triangular faces meeting at an apex, with 8 edges and 5 vertices.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Zahra says all prisms have the same number of faces. Is she right? Explain using a triangular prism and a hexagonal prism.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: No, Zahra is wrong. The number of faces depends on the base. A triangular prism has 5 faces, but a hexagonal prism has 8 faces (2 hexagons + 6 rectangles).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A net is made of 2 triangles and 3 rectangles. Which shape does it fold into? How many edges and vertices will it have?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A triangular prism; it has 9 edges and 6 vertices.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Ibrahim has 8 straws and 5 balls of clay. Which 3D shape can he build using all of them, and what is it called?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A square-based pyramid – 8 edges would need 8 straws, but with 8 straws and 5 clay balls he builds a square-based pyramid only if he has 8 edges and 5 vertices; the correct build is a square-based pyramid (8 edges, 5 vertices).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Understanding Pyramids.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A pyramid is another special 3D shape, but it is built in a very different way from a prism. A pyramid has: One base, which can be any flat polygon – a triangle, a square, a pentagon and so on.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Sorting and Classifying 3D Shapes.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Because there are so many 3D shapes, mathematicians like to sort them into groups. Sorting means putting shapes together that share a property. There is no single right way to sort – it depends on which property you choose. Here are some useful ways to sort the shapes you have met: Shapes with only flat faces (cube, cuboid, prisms, pyramids) versus shapes with at least one curved surface (sphere, cylinder, cone).

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, hold a real object in your hand while you read, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to describe a shape in a new way, give you extra practice, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Understanding 3D shapes helps you make sense of the world around you. Builders must know shapes to raise a strong house. Traders stack boxes that are cuboids so they fit neatly on the shelf. A potter shaping camel-milk jars is working with cylinders and spheres. When you can name a shape and describe its parts, you are learning the language that engineers, architects and designers use every day – and you are building the foundation for measuring volume and surface area in later years.

I can as you work, keep some real objects beside you: a tin, a box, a ball, a party hat, a dice. Mathematics with shapes is best learned by touching and turning. Run your finger along an edge. Press your fingertip on a corner. Feel the flat and curved surfaces. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here makes you a sharper, more confident mathematician.
I can identify and name common 3D shapes: cube, cuboid, sphere, cylinder, cone, prism and pyramid.
I can describe a 3D shape by its faces, edges and vertices.
I can explain the difference between a prism and a pyramid.
I can recognise 3D shapes in everyday objects around you.
I can sort and classify 3D shapes by their properties.
